home.metaTitle: OpenClienting — The open venture clienting knowledge base
home.metaDescription: Become a client of top startups that already solve your problem. Peer-validated problem templates and pilot playbooks. The open venture clienting knowledge base.
home.heroEyebrow: The open venture clienting knowledge base
home.heroSmeTitle2: top startups that solve your problem.
home.heroSmeSubtitle: Publish a problem, compare proven startup solutions against your explicit requirements, and pilot the best fit — using peer-validated templates and playbooks from SMEs who've been stuck on the same thing.
home.heroStartupSubtitle: Every published problem is a real company sourcing a proven solution. Match against explicit requirements, win structured pilots, and earn verified success badges that follow your solution across the platform.
home.exploreByIndustrySubtitle: Find real challenges specific to your sector.
home.featuredSuccessStorySectionSubtitle: A recent pilot that hit its success criteria.
home.forSmesDescription: Publish a problem, compare proven startup solutions, and pilot the best fit — without drafting an RFP from scratch or fielding a pile of cold pitches. Peer-validated templates, requirements, and pilot frameworks from SMEs who've already been stuck on the same thing.
home.forSmesFeature1Body: See which startups have closed pilots for problems like yours. Verified success reports beat pitch decks every time.
home.forSmesFeature2Body: Publish sensitive internal pain points without exposing your company — identity stays on our side for moderation only.
home.forSmesFeature3Body: Peer-validated problem descriptions, requirements, KPIs, and pilot playbooks you can adapt to your context — so you're not drafting an RFP from scratch.
home.forStartupsDescription: Every published problem is a real company actively sourcing a proven solution. Match against explicit requirements, win structured pilots, and land paying client engagements — not speculative early-adopter deals.
home.forStartupsFeature1Body: Each problem comes with upvote counts, peer-submitted requirements, and industry context — companies that already know what they need, not exploratory feedback sessions.
home.forStartupsFeature2Body: When a pilot closes, the company files a moderated success report. The badge follows your solution across the site and helps the next buyer pick you.
home.forStartupsFeature3Body: Skip accelerator panels and investor decks. These are companies ready to engage a vendor around a specific problem.
home.howItWorksSubtitle: From problem discovery to successful pilots — in three steps.
home.step1Body: Explore validated business challenges from SMEs across industries, complete with requirements and success criteria.
home.step2Body: Submit problem templates, add requirements, propose pilot frameworks, and share your domain expertise.
home.step3Body: Use structured pilot playbooks to test solutions. Report success stories and help the community learn.
home.recentProblemsSubtitle: Latest challenges submitted by the community.

ventureClienting.metaDescription: Venture clienting is how companies become clients of top startups that already solve their problem — without taking equity. OpenClienting is the open, crowdsourced knowledge base for it.
ventureClienting.title: What is venture clienting?
ventureClienting.lede: Venture clienting is how established companies work with top startups — by becoming their paying client. Not their investor, not their first-ever customer. The startup already has a working solution; the company adopts it through a normal vendor engagement. OpenClienting is the open, crowdsourced knowledge base behind it.
ventureClienting.section1Body: A company publishes a problem. Top startups that already have a solution propose how they'd pilot it. The best fit runs a short, structured pilot with clear success criteria. When it works, the company scales it up as a normal vendor engagement — a paying client relationship, not an equity stake and not a speculative first-customer experiment. No cap table, no board seat, no multi-year negotiation.
ventureClienting.section2Title: How is it different from corporate venture capital?
ventureClienting.section2Body: Corporate VC buys equity and hopes for a financial return years later. Venture clienting buys a solution — the company becomes a paying client of a startup whose technology already works. It's faster, cheaper, and outcome-oriented: a normal vendor relationship around a specific problem, not a speculative bet on an unproven team.
ventureClienting.section3Title: What companies get out of it
ventureClienting.section3Body: A published problem is a filter. Instead of fielding 50 cold pitches a month, you see the startups whose solutions already match your explicit requirements. Reuse peer-validated problem templates, KPIs, and pilot frameworks from companies who've already been stuck on the same thing — and publish anonymously if the pain is sensitive.
ventureClienting.section4Title: What startups get out of it
ventureClienting.section4Body: Every problem on the site is a real company actively sourcing a proven solution — not a VC pitch, not an accelerator application. Propose a pilot against explicit requirements and, when it lands, enter a normal paying client engagement plus a verified success badge that follows your solution across the platform.
ventureClienting.section5Body: Most venture clienting programs today are closed inside big-company innovation teams. The problem templates, requirements, and pilot playbooks they build get locked behind NDAs and never benefit anyone else. OpenClienting is the opposite bet: make the knowledge base open, let smaller SMEs tap into proven startup solutions, and let top startups be discovered by the companies that actually need them.

privacy.metaDescription: How we handle your personal data.

consent.title: Cookies
consent.body: We use strictly necessary cookies for authentication and a few basic preferences. With your consent we also load Google Tag Manager / Google Analytics to understand how visitors use our site.
consent.necessaryDescription: Required to keep you signed in and to remember basic preferences such as language, theme, and consent choice. Always active.
consent.analyticsDescription: Loads Google Tag Manager, which in turn loads Google Analytics 4. Helps us understand how visitors use the site. Only active after you opt in.
